chunk_summary: This section discusses pediatric food allergy mismanagement, focusing on mislabeling, overtreatment, and appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies using case-based vignettes.
learning_objectives: Understand the distinction between sensitization and true food allergy in pediatric cases.
Learn to manage food allergy mislabeling and overtreatment through targeted history and testing.
Develop strategies to restore dietary diversity while ensuring emergency readiness.
Recognize the role of dietitian input in addressing nutritional compromise during diagnostic work-ups.
key_facts: Positive specific IgE (sIgE) tests indicate sensitization, not necessarily clinical allergy.
Eczema naturally waxes and wanes, and improvement after food elimination is not proof of causality unless symptoms relapse on reintroduction.
Supervised oral food challenges (OFC) are critical for de-labeling low-probability food allergies.
Chronic abdominal pain without temporal food links requires evaluation for celiac disease and functional pain, not broad elimination diets.
Dietary eliminations should be time-bounded and based on clear immune phenotypes or diagnostic evidence.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: ‘Positive sIgE indicates sensitization, not allergy’ (Rational Use of Allergy Testing, IAP Action Plan 2026).
document_key_topics: Targeted diagnostic approaches for food allergy
Restoring dietary diversity and addressing nutritional compromise
